coredata.dc:title: An innovative carbon dots polarity probe based on intramolecular charge-transfer for visual monitoring of the total polar materials in frying oil
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Jiangbin
Yang, Wei
Liu, Yuanfa
coredata.dc:description: The presence of Total Polar Materials (TPM) in edible oils is a crucial indicator for assessing oil quality. It is of paramount importance to develop a rapid and dependable technique for monitoring polarity in frying oil. Sensitive polarity responsive fluorescence carbon dots (F-CDs) were synthesized by using p-phenylenediamine as precursors and 2-formylphenylboronic acid pinacol ester (2-FAPE) as a post-modifier. The construction of the fluorescent probe F-CDs involved a strong intramolecular charge-transfer (ICT) mechanism, with 2-FAPE serving as the electron-withdrawing fluorophore and the π-conjugated structure acting as a potent electron-donating group. A strong linear relationship was observed between the emission wavelength and the TPM value of frying oil within a range of 11% to 30%. Notably, the fluorescence color of the probe transitioned from blue to yellow under UV light at 365 nm as the TMP value increased. This study expands the range of sensing applications for CDs in food safety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Polarity
Intramolecular charge-transfer
Total polar materials
Frying oil

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of frying oil TPM

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Key Research and Development Program of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Key Research and Development Program of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Project of Jiangsu Provincial Center of Technology Innovation for Future Food

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Key Research and Development Program of China (2021YFD2100300) and Project of Jiangsu Provincial Center of Technology Innovation for Future Food (BM2020023) as well as Jiangsu Specially-Appointed Professor program.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of real-time and visual monitoring for TPM in frying oil by F-CDs fluorescence probe.

(a) TEM image of the F-CDs. Inset: HR-TEM image of F-CDs. (b) Particle size distribution measured by TEM. (c) XRD pattern of the F-CDs. (d) Raman spectrum of the F-CDs. (e) FT-IR spectra of the P-CDs and F-CDs. (f) XPS spectrum of F-CDs. High resolution XPS spectra and the corresponding fitting curves of (g) C 1 s, (h) N 1 s and (i) B 1 s of Y-CDs.

(a) The maximum emission spectrum and best excitation normalized spectrum of F-CDs. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of P-CDs and F-CDs. The emission spectra (c) and normalized emission spectra (d) of F-CDs in different solvents.

(a) The emission spectra of F-CDs in different H2O/1,4-dioxane ratios (0% − 100%). (b) The linear relationship between the maximum emission wavelength and the solvent's Δƒ under H2O/1,4-dioxane ratios (0% − 100%). (c) The emission spectra of F-CDs in different H2O/1,4-dioxane ratios (0% − 10%). (d) The linear relationship between the maximum emission wavelength and the solvent's Δƒ under H2O/1,4-dioxane ratios (0% − 10%). (e) Fluorescence intensity of F-CDs under 100% water with different pH values from 2.0 to 8.0. (f) Fluorescence intensity of F-CDs under 100% water at different temperatures.

DFT calculations. (a) Calculated HOMO/LUMO distribution of P-CDs and F-CDs. (b) Hole (blue) & elelctron (green) distribution of P-CDs and F-CDs. (c) Hole & electron overlap heatmap of P-CDs and F-CDs. The horizontal coordinate is the serial number of non‑hydrogen atoms, starting from 1. This graph describes the contribution of each non‑hydrogen atom to the hole, the electron, and the overlap of the two by color. A red matrix element corresponds to a high value. (d) D index: Hole & electron distance; Sr index: hole & electron overlap function; t index: hole & electron separation function. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) The normalized emission spectra of F-CDs fluorescence probe for frying oil with different TPM value. (b) The linear relationship between the maximum emission wavelength and the TPM value. (c) The fluorescence intensity under different oil temperature. (d) The fluorescent photos of F-CDs probe adding frying oil with different TPM value under 365 nm UV lamps. (d) The TPM-dependent changes in ratio of green-to-red (G/R) values. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Detecting TPM in edible oil samples based on probe F-CDs (n = 3).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Average values are from three independent measurements at the same conditions.

head.ce:title: An innovative carbon dots polarity probe based on intramolecular charge-transfer for visual monitoring of the total polar materials in frying oil

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Future Food (Bai Ma) Research Institute, 111 Baima Road, Nanjing 211200, Jiangsu, People's Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Future Food (Bai Ma) Research Institute, 111 Baima Road, Nanjing 211200, Jiangsu, People's Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The presence of Total Polar Materials (TPM) in edible oils is a crucial indicator for assessing oil quality. It is of paramount importance to develop a rapid and dependable technique for monitoring polarity in frying oil. Sensitive polarity responsive fluorescence carbon dots (F-CDs) were synthesized by using p-phenylenediamine as precursors and 2-formylphenylboronic acid pinacol ester (2-FAPE) as a post-modifier. The construction of the fluorescent probe F-CDs involved a strong intramolecular charge-transfer (ICT) mechanism, with 2-FAPE serving as the electron-withdrawing fluorophore and the π-conjugated structure acting as a potent electron-donating group. A strong linear relationship was observed between the emission wavelength and the TPM value of frying oil within a range of 11% to 30%. Notably, the fluorescence color of the probe transitioned from blue to yellow under UV light at 365 nm as the TMP value increased. This study expands the range of sensing applications for CDs in food safety.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Edible oils are essential to our diet and contributing significantly to of our health. Complex chemical reactions that occur during the storage and processing of edible oils can generate polar compounds, which in turn degrade the quality and nutritional value of the oils (Chen et al., 2021). Over the years, numerous studies have been conducted on the health implications of frying oil and its polar components. Consuming substandard oil can lead to various health complications, including obesity, cardiovascular and cerebrovascular diseases, and even an increased risk of cancer (Huangfu & Feng, 2021). To ensure the quality of edible oils, several countries have set discard points for total polar materials (TPM) between 24% and 27%, with China setting the limit at 27% (Upadhyay et al., 2017). Therefore, the TPM value has become a widely analyzed parameter for assessing edible oil quality. Currently, several methods are used to monitor polarity, including silica gel column chromatography, size-exclusion chromatography (SEC), nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR), capacitive measurement, and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (Chen et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2015). However, these traditional methods are time-consuming and do not allow for real-time monitoring of mitophagy. Therefore, it is imperative to devise a straightforward and efficient approach for the swift identification of TPM in edible oils. This is not only crucial for monitoring in the food industry but also for safeguarding human health.

Recently, the fluorescence analytical method has become popular due to its quickly, sensitively, and visually detect analytes (Zhang et al., 2021). This fluorescent response can be captured and analyzed using portable devices such as smartphones, thereby eliminating the need for bulky instrumentation (Feng et al., 2021). Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), a type of carbon-based nanoparticle, are widely used as promising fluorescent probes. Their unique optical, chemical, and biological characteristics make them ideal for applications in bioimaging and the detection of harmful substances (Li et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2023). Several studies have successfully created CDs probes that exhibit sensitivity to polarity (Hu et al., 2022; Shi et al., 2020). However, many of these CDs rely on a singular emission wavelength, resulting in lower sensitivity to minor polarity variations (Hu et al., 2022). Additionally, the discovery of these polarity-sensitive CDs has predominantly been achieved through trial and error methods. The unclear emission source and lack of logical strategies for modifying the optical properties have resulted from the absence of a systematic design approach. The absence of systematic design approach presents a considerable obstacle to pursuing more detailed studies on fluorescent carbon dots. The process of intramolecular charge-transfer (ICT) has been found to be impacted by the surrounding environment, with the ICT process of a molecule being modulated by polarity (Misra & Bhattacharyya, 2018). Additionally, enhancing the ICT effect can induce a redshift in the fluorescence molecular emission wavelength (Sun et al., 2020). Previous research has shown that photoluminescence observed in CDs is linked to the “extrinsic” emission of functional groups such as CO and CN (Li et al., 2021). Therefore, the introduction of functional groups with electron-donating or withdrawing ability can create a new energy state and absorption domain.

Herein, polarity-sensitive fluorescence carbon dots (F-CDs) were synthesized by using 2-formylphenylboronic acid pinacol ester (2-FAPE) as a post-modifier. The probe showed high sensitivity to polarity. An excellent linear relationship was observed between the fluorescent emission wavelength of F-CDs and the polarity parameter (Δƒ) from 0% water (Δƒ = 0.03) to 10% water (Δƒ = 0.229). Through data analysis, the F-CDs were composed of a large π-conjugated structure containing sp3- and sp2-hybridized carbon atoms, which was modified by 2-FAPE, which has a strong electron-withdrawing effect. Theoretical investigations have demonstrated that the F-CDs possessed both electron-donating and electron-withdrawing groups. The transition from the ground state (S0) to the first excited state (S1) was attributed to ICT, where 2-FAPE passivation played a crucial role. The introduction of a surface modifier to the CDs created an electron deficiency, which significantly enhanced the ICT effect and provided F-CDs with an excellent sensitivity to changes in polarity. Finally, the F-CDs can be used to monitoring the TPM value of frying oil within a range of 11% to 30% due to their high polarity sensitivity. Meanwhile, the fluorescence probe could exhibit an obvious color response (blue to chartreuse) with an excellent sensitivity and good stability. Therefore, the synthesized F-CDs fluorescence probe exhibits great potential as a viable alternative for real-time, non-destructive, and visual monitoring of TPM values in frying oil.

P-phenylenediamine, 2-formylphenylboronic acid pinacol ester (2-FAPE) and ethyl alcohol were acquired from Macklin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ethanol, dichloromethane, 1,4-dioxane, methyl alcohol and acetone were purchased from Titan Technology Co., (Shanghai, China). The edible oils were acquired from a supermarket in the local area. All used chemicals are of analytical grade in this synthesis and analysis experiment. Distilled water was employed throughout the entirety of the experiment.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were collected from JEM-ARM200F field-emission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). The Raman spectrum was obtained using LabRAM HR Evolution micro-Raman spectrometer (HORIBA Jobin Yvon S.A.S., France). A D8 ADVANCE (XRD, Bruker, Germany) was used to conduct the X-ray diffractometer. The UV-1750 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) was employed to carry out the UV–visible absorption spectra analysis. The fluorescence spectra were gathered using a HITACHI F-7000 spectrophotometer (HITACHI, Japan). A Vertex 70 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) was employed for scanning Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR). X-ray optoelectronic energy spectrum (XPS) data was provided by a Thermo Escalab 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA).

Briefly, 0.2 g of p-phenylenediamine was incorporated into a 20 mL of ethanol solution and dissolved by sonication. The resulting solution was then placed into Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for a period of 12 h. Upon the mixture returning to room temperature, it was subjected to purification through silica column chromatography, leading to the production of P-CDs. The F-CDs were synthesized by connecting P-CDs and 2-FAPE through Schiff base formation. Specifically, the obtained P-CDs (0.5 mL, 10 mg mL−1) and 2-FAPE (5 mM) were dissolved in methanol (10 mL), and the solution was stirred at room temperature for a duration of 30 min. The resulting mixture was subsequently evaporated, resulting in crude products. These crude products were subjected to further purification using silica gel column chromatography, with an eluent composed of dichloromethane and methanol in a volume ratio of 10:1. A stock solution was formulated in ethanol with a density of 10 mg mL−1.

All quantum chemistry calculations were performed by the Gaussian 16 program package (Frisch et al., 2016). The geometry optimization of F-CDs model was conducted in the gas phase through B3LYP/6-31G(d) level of theory (Geng et al., 2022). The excited-state calculation in conjunction with the conductor-like polarized continuum model (C-PCM) framework in an ethanol solvent was used for simulation of UV-vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra at the B3LYP/6-31G(d) level (Liu et al., 2020). The electronic structure analysis was carried out with the Multiwfn 3.7(dev) code (Lu & Chen, 2012). The isosurface maps of frontier molecular orbitals (FMOs) were visualized using Visual Molecular Dynamics (VMD) software, which processed the files exported by Multiwfn (Humphrey et al., 1996).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of frying oil TPM
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence monitoring procedure for frying oil TPM was carried out as follows. A series of edible oil with different TPM content were obtained through a process of continuous frying of French fries at a temperature of 180 °C. The TPM value in each oil sample was subsequently measured using a Testo 270 device. In instances where the TPM value exceeded 30%, the frying oil was mixed with fresh oil in varying proportions to yield oil samples with distinct TPM values. These values included 13%, 15%, 20%, 27%, and 30%. Firstly, the edible oil was diluted tenfold with acetone through ultrasound treatment for 10 min. Subsequently, 40 μL of the F-CDs stock solution was added to the acetone solution of frying oil (500 μL), and the fluorescence spectra were recorded. After two seconds of blending, the fluorescence spectra were recorded to establish the relationship between TPM value and maximum emission wavelength. Finally, images of fluorescence were obtained using a camera on a smartphone and their RGB values were evaluated using a color recognition application on the same device.

Fluorescent probes for monitoring polarity have been widely designed using intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) fluorophores. These ICT-based fluorescence scaffolds typically consist of an electron-donating group conjugation with an electron-withdrawing group (Tian et al., 2021). In the Fig. 1(a), the P-CDs were synthesized from p-phenylenediamine using a solvothermal method, then 2-FAPE as surface modifier was used to prepare F-CDs through Schiff base formation. In the compound 2-FAPE, the boron atom, situated in close proximity to the aldehyde group, serves as a catalyst in the Schiff bases synthesis process (Chatterjee et al., 2021; Draganov et al., 2015). The design of the fluorescent probe F-CDs is based on the formation of Schiff bases, which modify the graphitized carbon cores. This modification introduced strong electron-absorbing groups, resulting in F-CDs that possess a powerful ICT effect in response to changes in polarity. The structure character of F-CDs was investigated through characterization equipment such as transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Raman spectroscopy. As shown in the TEM image (Fig. 2a, b), the F-CDs were uniformly dispersed and presented circular shapes with an average diameter of 2.56 nm. The lattice spacing discerned in the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image is 0.21 nm, which aligns with the (100) plane of graphene (Zhan et al., 2023). The XRD pattern of the F-CDs in the Fig. 2c exhibited an obvious sharp peak centered at 14.2°, and two broad weak peaks at ≈26.8° and 41.5°, revealing the occurrence of graphitization Guo et al., 2022). The broad peak at 26.8° and 41.5° corresponds to the (002) and (100) facet with an interlamellar spacing of 0.34 and 0.21 nm, respectively (Xu et al., 2021). As depicted in Fig. 2d, the Raman spectrum of F-CDs displayed two peaks at 1350 and 1586 cm−1. These peaks are representative of the disordered (D band) and ordered graphite (G band) carbon structures, respectively, signifying the simultaneous presence of sp3- and sp2- hybridized carbon in F-CDs. The ID/IG ratio is observed to be 0.93, which signified a high degree of graphitization in the F-CDs (Li et al., 2021). To gain a more detailed insight, both Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were utilized to ascertain the chemical composition and surface functional groups present in the F-CDs. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2e) confirmed the presence of a π-conjugated structure according to the aromatic CC stretching vibrations (1633 cm−1) and aromatic CH in-plane bending vibrations (1140 cm−1) (Jia et al., 2019). The absorption band of considerable width observed at 3421 cm−1 can be ascribed to the stretching vibration of NH situated on the F-CDs surface (Hu et al., 2022). The peaks observed at 3000–2850 and 1384 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum are indicative of the CH and CN stretching vibrations, respectively (Chen et al., 2023; Ding et al., 2016). To verify the successful modification of 2-FAPE, the FT-IR spectrum of the P-CDs was meanwhile observed and compared with that of the F-CDs. Specifically, F-CDs exhibited two unique absorption peaks at 1348 and 1108 cm−1, which are attributed to the BO and CB stretching vibrations, respectively (Guo et al., 2022; Shen & Xia, 2014). The full XPS spectra (Fig. 2f and S1) revealed that F-CDs exhibited a B 1 s peak (193.5 eV) compared with P-CDs, in addition to peaks for C 1 s (284.3 eV), N 1 s (399.8 eV), and O 1 s (532.4 eV). The high-resolution C 1 s XPS curve (Fig. 2g) was deconvoluted into four distinct peaks, corresponding to C=C/C-C (284.5 eV), CN (285.7 eV), CB (283.9 eV), and CO (287.6 eV) bonds (Y.-Z. Guo et al., 2022). The N 1 s XPS spectra (Fig. 2h) showed two peaks representing C=N-C (398.9 eV) and NH (399.8 eV) bonds. Additionally, the B 1 s XPS spectra in Fig. 2i showcased two distinct binding states, namely BC (191.2 eV) and BO (192.8 eV). This observation substantiated the successful synthesis of F-CDs (Arcudi et al., 2021). The XPS findings align with the above FTIR analysis, which demonstrated that during the process of high temperature solvent-thermal treatment, an elimination reaction takes place between -NH2 and the phenyl ring within the precursor molecules. This reaction results in the formation of a single bond that interconnects the precursor molecules. As this reaction advances, additional reactions such as dehydrogenation transpire, leading to the creation of an extensive π-conjugated structure. It is important to note that this structure is susceptible to oxidation by hydroxyl groups. To summarize, the F-CDs are composed of large π-conjugated structure which contain sp3- and sp2-hybridized carbon, and modified by 2-FAPE possessing strong electron-withdrawing effect.

The photophysical properties of F-CDs in ethanol solution were explored through UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. It can be depicted from Fig. 3a, the best excitation wavelength for F-CDs in ethanol was found to be at 435 nm, while the maximum emission wavelength was recorded at 532 nm. The F-CDs solution exhibited to be transparent under room light and appeared intense bright chartreuse fluorescence upon 365 nm ultraviolet (UV) excitation (inset in Fig. 3a). As exhibited in Fig. 3b, two distinct absorption peaks were observed in the UV–visible spectrum of P-CDs, specifically around 266 nm and 310 nm. These peaks are indicative of the π–π* and n–π* transitions associated with aromatic domains and CN bands, respectively. However, a pronounced absorption band centered at 410 nm was observed for F-CDs, which suggested the appearance of different surface states compared with P-CDs (Bao et al., 2011; Nie et al., 2014). The result further verified the post-modification using 2-FAPE was carried out successfully.

The fluorescence spectra of F-CDs were captured across a spectrum of solvents with varying polarities, in order to evaluate their sensitivity to changes in polarity (Fig. 3c). As depicted in Fig. 3d, the normalized emission spectra indicated a red-shift in the fluorescence wavelength of the F-CDs as the polarity of solvent intensifies. To demonstrate the polarity sensitivity, the FL spectra of F-CDs were examined in a mixed system of H2O/dioxane with different ratio (fw). As shown in Fig. 4a, the maximum emission wavelength exhibited a red-shift, transitioning from an initial 501 nm to a subsequent 535 nm. This change also prompted an approximately 77-fold decrease in the fluorescence intensity of F-CDs when the solvent was switched from dioxane to water. The fluorescent emission wavelength of F-CDs showed a linear relationship with the polarity parameter (Δƒ) in the Fig. 4b. Further evidence of the polarity sensitivity was observed when an addition of mere 1% water led to a 1.49-fold reduction in fluorescence intensity and induced a red-shift of 10 nm in emission wavelength of F-CDs (Fig. 4c). Meanwhile, a linear relationship was observed between the fluorescent emission wavelength of F-CDs and the polarity parameter (Δƒ) from 0% water (Δƒ = 0.03) to 10% water (Δƒ = 0.229) in the Fig. 4d. The wonderful sensitivity and linearity towards polarity can be ascribed to the process of post-modification. This process may incorporate a strong electron-withdrawing group, thereby considerably augmenting the Internal Charge Transfer (ICT) effect of F-CDs. These findings suggested that F-CDs are highly sensitive to even slight changes in the polarity of their surroundings. In light of the impact of TPM on frying oil pH and the fact that polarity-sensitive molecules often exhibit pH-sensitive behavior, which can significantly affect the monitoring of polarity based on emission maximum. In a purely aqueous environment, the fluorescence emission spectrum of F-CDs was investigated across a pH spectrum from 2.0 to 8.0. We found that the fluorescence was unchanged on pH in the range of 2.0–8.0 in the Fig. 4e. The exceptional ability of F-CDs to resist interference is crucial for their successful implementation in practical scenarios, particularly when analyzing complex food samples. The presence of common interfering saline ions (Fe3+, Al3+, Cr3+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Hg2+, K+, Na+, Ag+) did not result in any changes in the fluorescence intensity (Fig. 4f), demonstrating that the F-CDs possessed remarkable stability. The results indicated the potential suitability of F-CDs as a polarity probe in complicated frying oil environments.

To obtain optimal experimental results, the response time and reaction temperature were optimized. As shown in Fig. S2a, the fluorescence intensity remained stable over the period 0–60 min after the probe solution was completely mixed with water. The probe demonstrated exceptional response speed and stability, indicating its capability for rapid and consistent measurement of the TPM content in edible oils. However, the fluorescent performance was compromised at high temperatures due to the limited thermal stability of CDs. Consequently, a series of comparative experiments were conducted within a temperature range of 10 to 35 °C, considering the dynamic nature of room temperature fluctuations. The findings depicted in Fig. S2b demonstrated consistent fluorescence intensities throughout the temperature variation, indicating minimal impact on the outcomes within this range. Therefore, the fluorescent monitoring of TPM was conducted at room temperature.

In this study, a large π-conjugated structure carbon dot post-modified by 2-FAPE possessing strong electron-withdrawing ability was constructed to achieve multicolor emission triggered by polarity-responsive behavior. As polarity increased, the fluorescence spectrum exhibited a redshift, which may be considered to result from the intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) process occurring within F-CDs. To support this speculation, we measured the zeta potentials of the F-CDs in solutions with varying polarities. As illustrated in Fig. S3a, the zeta potentials exhibited a decrease upon the addition of water. This phenomenon can be attributed to the heightened electron cloud density of F-CDs in solvents with high polarity, thereby facilitating emission with longer wavelengths. Meanwhile, the fluorescence lifetime decay curve of F-CDs was also measured for different volume ratios of water (Fig. S3b). As the water volume fraction in the mixture increased, a decay in fluorescence lifetime of F-CDs was observed.

Additionally, we used Gaussian 16 software and employed density functional theory (DFT) to systematically investigated the optimal structures and electron excitation characteristics of CDs. According to the structural characterization, it was inferred that the π-conjugated structure comprised mainly of sp2-hybridized carbon cores, interspersed with minor regions of sp3-hybridized carbon. As a result, we formulated two models of CDs for DFT analysis, which included CDs with an aromatic structure (P-CDs) and P-CDs that were modified with 2-FAPE (F-CDs). The optimal structures of these models were illustrated in Fig. S4. The UV–visible absorption and fluorescence spectra (Fig. S5) of model CDs were simulated through Time-Dependent DFT (TD-DFT) methods. The outcomes are consistent with the experimental data, thereby validating the accuracy of the CDs models. The presence of charge transfer excited state can be inferred by evaluating the spatial separation between the electron cloud density distributions in the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) (“Book Reviews,”, 2002; Haberhauer et al., 2016). Further, we calculated HOMO/LUMO distribution, hole-electron analysis and quantification of molecular orbital to systematically study the impact of electron-withdrawing groups of 2-FAPE on the electron excitation characteristics of CDs. As shown in Fig. 5a, the HOMO and LUMO of P-CDs were almost evenly distributed across the conjugated π-surface. However, the HOMO of F-CDs was distributed on graphitized cores, while the LUMO was concentrated on the C=N-C group (Schiff base cluster) and 2-FAPE. The distinct separation between the HOMO and LUMO imparts a strong ICT character to the F-CDs probe in the S1 state and enhances the polarity of molecule, thereby exhibiting the polarity-sensitive nature of its emissions (Yang et al., 2023). Additionally, it has been stated that the existence of a charge transfer excited state in a π-conjugated system can result in a red-shift of λem. It can be seen that F-CDs and P-CDs had similar HOMO levels of −4.95 eV and −4.79 eV, respectively. The modification of 2-FAPE narrowed the energy gaps (from 5.37 eV to 4.25 eV) between HOMO and LUMO, suggesting the ability of ICT was enhanced. From a chemical perspective, F-CDs can be perceived as extraordinary aromatic entities, endowed with both electron donor and acceptor. Fig. 5d illustrated an increased dipole moment in F-CDs compared to P-CDs, suggesting that 2-FAPE could potentially cause greater polarization on the CD surface. According to the survey of hole-electron (Fig. 5b), the distribution of holes and electrons in P-CDs was almost identical, while in F-CDs, the distribution of electrons significantly shifted towards the 2-FAPE. Based on the quantitative molecular orbital analysis in Table S1, there were primarily two transitions from ground state to an excited state for P-CDs: H- > L (76.7%) and H- > L + 2 (11.0%) (H and L denote HOMO and LUMO, respectively). Meanwhile, hole-electron analysis (Fig. 5d) showed distance (D) index = 0.428 Å, hole & electron overlap function (Sr) = 0.705 a.u. and hole & electron separation function (t) = −1.397 a.u.), the S0–S1 excitation of P-CDs is attributed to local excitation (LE) excitation and intermolecular charge transfer (ICT). For F-CDs, the S0–S1excitation (D index = 3.522 Å, Sr = 0.679 a.u. and t = 0.568 a.u.) is derived from H- > L (89.7%), indicating that the S0–S1 excitation of F-CDs involves intermolecular charge transfer (ICT). Upon further analysis of atomic contributions to holes and electrons (Fig. 5c), it is found that for P-CDs the holes were almost contributed by the N atom (13, 26) and C atom (2, 24) of NC in the π-conjugated structure according to Fig. S4 atom number. The electrons were mainly contributed by the C atom (3, 23, 24) of NC in the π-conjugated structure, but other regions also have significant contributions. For F-CDs，the holes were primarily derived from the N atom (29) of NC in the π-conjugated structure and a benzene ring (36–37, 39–40, 42) connected to C=N-C group. Upon excitation, the electrons were transferred to the N atom (43) and C atom (7) of the C=N-C group, as well as to the other end of the benzene ring (2, 4, 6) that is attached to the C=N-C group. Therefore, it is adequate to demonstrate that the electron-donating and electron-accepting groups in F-CDs are interconnected through the C=N-C group.

After achieving high sensitivity to polarity, we proceeded to assess the practical utility of the visual fluorescent probe for TPM analysis in frying oil. An essential criterion for a new method is its correlation with established methods. In the monitoring of polarity in frying oil, the significance lies in the correlation between the fluorescence spectrum and the TPM value as measured by Testo 270. We investigated the analytical performance of the fluorescent sensing method under optimized conditions, using palm oil as our subject of study. Firstly, a series of oil samples with different TPM values were obtained as standard oil samples for the establishment of calibration curves in the polarity analysis of frying oil. The titration experiments were then conducted by adding a frying oil solution with different TPM values into the probe system. As illustrated in Fig. 6a, the increase in the TPM value of palm oil induced a red-shift in the fluorescent spectrum of the sensor solution. A strong linear relationship (R2 = 0.9907) was observed between the emission wavelength and the TPM value of frying oil within a range of 11% to 30% (Fig. 6b). As shown in Fig. 6d that as the TMP value increased, the fluorescence color of the probe transitioned from blue to yellow under UV light at 365 nm. This perceptible alteration in color can be readily observed by the naked eye, eliminating the necessity for intricate analytical apparatus. Moreover, this image facilitated the quantification of TPM by determining the green-to-red (G/R) emission color ratio, which could be easily accomplished using a color recognition application on a smartphone. A strong linear relationship was observed between the G/R ratio and the TPM value of frying oil (R2 = 0.9884) (Fig. 6e). According to the data presented in Table S2, the analysis performance of the F-CDs sensor prepared herein exhibited similarities or even surpass those of previously reported methods.

To further assess the suitability of fluorescence probe in practical oil sample, the standard addition method was employed on three oil samples. The TPM content was determined using the probe F-CDs and compared with standard values obtained through national standard method (flash column chromatography, FCC). As demonstrated in Table 1 , the results obtained from spiked samples using this method exhibited a high degree of similarity to those acquired through the FCC technique, indicating the excellent accuracy and precision of this method in comparison with the FCC approach. These oil samples exhibited satisfactory recoveries within the range of 101.33–109.96%. Additionally, the stability of fluorescence probe system was assessed by adding oil at different temperatures (20–45 °C) during the pretreatment process. As shown in Fig. 6c, the oil temperature did not disturb the fluorescence response of the probe. The cumulative evidences suggested that the F-CDs fluorescence probe can effectively serve as a real-time, non-destructive, and visual indicator for monitoring the TPM value in frying oil.

A novel method for monitoring the TPM value in frying oil has been developed. This method utilizes the high sensitivity response of F-CDs to polarity, which induces changes in the fluorescence emission of the probe. F-CDs were prepared using 2-FAPE as a post-modifier, which endowed them with excellent sensitivity towards polarity due to their strong ICT effect. Notably, the F-CDs can be used to monitor the TPM value of frying oil within a range of 11% to 30% due to their high sensitivity. The F-CDs exhibited good stability at different temperatures and were able to visualize the detection of TPM values through the changes in fluorescence color. Data analysis revealed that the F-CDs were composed of a large π-conjugated structure containing sp3- and sp2-hybridized carbon atoms, which had been modified by 2-FAPE possessing a strong electron-withdrawing effect. Theoretical investigations have demonstrated that the F-CDs possessed both electron-donating and electron-withdrawing groups, its S0–S1 excitation was assigned to ICT, where 2-FAPE passivation played a key role. This methodology has significant potential for practical implementation in food analysis, and could potentially stimulate further innovative designs for detecting vegetable oils.
Jiangbin Xu: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Wei Yang: Validation, Formal analysis. Yuanfa Liu: Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Key Research and Development Program of China (2021YFD2100300) and Project of Jiangsu Provincial Center of Technology Innovation for Future Food (BM2020023) as well as Jiangsu Specially-Appointed Professor program.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2024.139770.
